expanded_description: The digestive system is a continuous muscular tube extending from mouth to anus, specialized for mechanical and chemical breakdown of food, nutrient absorption, and waste elimination through coordinated enzymatic, hormonal, and neural regulation.

core_concept.explanation: Food undergoes mechanical processing and enzymatic hydrolysis as it travels through specialized compartments; nutrients are absorbed primarily in the small intestine via transport mechanisms optimized for each molecular class, while waste is compacted and eliminated.

image.caption: Alimentary canal from mouth to anus with accessory organs (liver, gallbladder, pancreas)

sections.section_title: Digestive Architecture: From Ingestion to Elimination

image.caption: Regional specialization: oral cavity (ingestion), stomach (storage/mixing), small intestine (digestion/absorption), large intestine (water recovery/elimination)

sections.introduction: The digestive system transforms complex dietary macromolecules into absorbable monomers through a precisely coordinated sequence of mechanical and chemical processes. Each organ contributes specialized functions: the mouth initiates digestion via mastication and salivary amylase; the stomach provides acidic protein denaturation; the small intestine completes enzymatic hydrolysis and absorbs nutrients; the large intestine recovers water and forms feces. Understanding this integrated system requires appreciating both anatomical specialization and physiological regulation.

introduction_image.caption: Sequential processing of food with key enzymes and transport mechanisms at each stage

key_concept.definition: Digestion is the enzymatic hydrolysis of macromolecules: carbohydrates $\rightarrow$ monosaccharides, proteins $\rightarrow$ amino acids, lipids $\rightarrow$ fatty acids + monoglycerides, nucleic acids $\rightarrow$ nucleotides, followed by absorptive transport across the intestinal epithelium.
key_concept.context: This principle explains why digestive enzymes are compartment-specific (salivary amylase in mouth, pepsin in stomach, pancreatic enzymes in duodenum) and why absorption mechanisms differ by nutrient class (SGLT1 for glucose, peptide transporters for di/tripeptides, micellar solubilization for lipids).

image.caption: General hydrolysis reactions for carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids with enzyme examples

classifications.value: Physical breakdown increasing surface area for enzymatic action
classifications.behavior: Reduces particle size, mixes food with secretions, and controls transit time to optimize enzymatic efficiency

examples.explanation: Peristaltic waves originating in gastric body mix chyme with gastric juice; pyloric sphincter regulates emptying of particles <2 mm

image.caption: Peristaltic mixing waves and pyloric regulation of gastric emptying

examples.explanation: Rhythmic localized contractions mix chyme with pancreatic enzymes and bile while exposing nutrients to absorptive surface

image.caption: Segmentation contractions enhancing mixing and absorption efficiency

classifications.value: Catalytic hydrolysis of specific macromolecular bonds
classifications.behavior: Enzymes exhibit substrate specificity and optimal pH ranges: salivary amylase (pH 6.7-7.0), pepsin (pH 1.5-2.5), pancreatic enzymes (pH 7.5-8.5)

examples.explanation: Pepsin (stomach) cleaves internal peptide bonds; trypsin/chymotrypsin (duodenum) further hydrolyze; brush-border peptidases complete digestion to amino acids and di/tripeptides

image.caption: Sequential protease action from stomach to intestinal brush border

examples.explanation: Bile salts emulsify large lipid droplets into micelles, increasing surface area for pancreatic lipase action: $\text{Triglyceride} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{lipase}} 2\text{Fatty acids} + \text{Monoglyceride}$

image.caption: Bile salt micelle formation enabling lipase access to triglyceride substrates

classifications.value: Specialized membrane transporters moving digestion products into blood or lymph
characteristics: Secondary active transport: SGLT1 for glucose/galactose
Facilitated diffusion: GLUT2, GLUT5 for monosaccharides
Micellar diffusion: fatty acids and monoglycerides enter enterocytes passively
classifications.behavior: Transport mechanisms match nutrient chemistry: polar molecules use protein-mediated transport; lipids diffuse through membranes after micellar solubilization

examples.explanation: Sodium-glucose linked transporter uses $\text{Na}^+$ gradient (maintained by Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase) to co-transport glucose against its concentration gradient: $\text{Glucose}_{lumen} + \text{Na}^+_{lumen} \rightarrow \text{Glucose}_{cell} + \text{Na}^+_{cell}$

image.caption: Secondary active transport of glucose coupled to sodium gradient

examples.explanation: Fatty acids and monoglycerides diffuse into enterocytes, are re-esterified to triglycerides, packaged with apolipoproteins into chylomicrons, and exocytosed into lacteals for lymphatic transport

image.caption: Intracellular processing of lipids for lymphatic transport

principles.statement: Digestive enzymes function optimally at pH values matching their anatomical location: salivary amylase (neutral), pepsin (acidic), pancreatic enzymes (alkaline)
principles.application: Explains why gastric acid is essential for protein digestion and why pancreatic bicarbonate neutralizes chyme for intestinal enzyme activity
principles.example: Pepsinogen activation: $\text{Pepsinogen} + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{Pepsin} + \text{peptide fragment}$; autocatalytic at pH < 3
principles.analogy: Like specialized tools for different workshop environments: acid-resistant enzymes for stomach 'workshop', alkaline-optimized enzymes for intestinal 'workshop'

image.caption: Activity curves of key digestive enzymes across pH range showing compartment-specific adaptation

principles.statement: The small intestine maximizes absorptive capacity through hierarchical folding: plicae circulares ($\times 3$), villi ($\times 10$), microvilli ($\times 20$), yielding ~600-fold surface area increase
principles.application: Explains efficiency of nutrient absorption despite short transit time (~3-6 hours); underlies pathology in celiac disease (villous atrophy reduces surface area)
principles.example: Total absorptive area: $\text{Length} \times \text{Circumference} \times \text{Amplification} \approx 6\text{m} \times 0.04\text{m} \times 600 \approx 144 \text{ m}^2$ (approaching tennis court size)
principles.analogy: Like unfolding a crumpled paper: same material but vastly increased surface for interaction

image.caption: Plicae circulares, villi, and microvilli creating massive absorptive surface area

principles.statement: Digestive processes are regulated by enteric nervous system reflexes and gastrointestinal hormones (gastrin, CCK, secretin, GIP) responding to luminal contents
principles.application: Explains cephalic phase (sight/smell of food triggers gastric secretion), gastric phase (distension/proteins stimulate acid), and intestinal phase (fats/acids trigger pancreatic secretion and gallbladder contraction)
principles.example: Fat in duodenum $\rightarrow$ I-cells release CCK $\rightarrow$ pancreatic enzyme secretion + gallbladder contraction + gastric emptying inhibition
principles.analogy: Like a smart factory control system: sensors detect raw material properties and adjust machinery settings accordingly

image.alt: Neural and hormonal control of digestion
image.caption: Integration of enteric reflexes and GI hormones coordinating digestive secretions and motility

components.definition: Enterocytes are the primary absorptive epithelial cells lining the small intestine, featuring apical microvilli (brush border) with embedded digestive enzymes and transporters
components.equation: $\text{Net Absorption Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Surface Area} \times \text{Transporter Density} \times \Delta C}{\text{Membrane Resistance}}$
components.interpretation: Enterocytes maintain polarity: apical membrane faces lumen with digestive enzymes (lactase, peptidases) and nutrient transporters; basolateral membrane contains Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase and facilitative transporters moving nutrients to blood; tight junctions regulate paracellular permeability

image.caption: Polarized enterocyte with apical microvilli, brush-border enzymes, and basolateral transport mechanisms

categories.description: Hydrolysis of polysaccharides and disaccharides to monosaccharides followed by transporter-mediated absorption
categories.detailed_mechanism: Salivary/pancreatic $\alpha$-amylase cleaves $\alpha$-1,4-glycosidic bonds producing maltose, maltotriose, $\alpha$-limit dextrins; brush-border enzymes (maltase, sucrase, lactase) complete hydrolysis; SGLT1 cotransports glucose/galactose with Na⁺; GLUT5 facilitates fructose diffusion; GLUT2 exports all monosaccharides to blood

examples.explanation: Lactase deficiency leaves lactose undigested; osmotic effect draws water into lumen; colonic bacteria ferment lactose producing gas (H₂, CO₂, CH₄) causing bloating, cramps, diarrhea

image.caption: Consequences of lactase deficiency on intestinal osmolarity and bacterial fermentation

categories.description: Proteolytic cascade converting dietary proteins to absorbable amino acids and small peptides
categories.detailed_mechanism: Gastric pepsin initiates hydrolysis at pH 1.5-2.5; pancreatic trypsin/chymotrypsin/carboxypeptidases further degrade; brush-border aminopeptidases/dipeptidases complete digestion; PEPT1 cotransports di/tripeptides with H⁺; amino acid transporters (multiple systems for neutral, basic, acidic, imino acids) absorb monomers

examples.explanation: Di/tripeptide absorption via PEPT1 is faster than free amino acid uptake; intracellular peptidases hydrolyze peptides, maintaining concentration gradient for continued uptake

image.caption: H⁺-coupled peptide cotransport and intracellular hydrolysis enhancing amino acid absorption efficiency

categories.description: Emulsification, enzymatic hydrolysis, micellar solubilization, and chylomicron-mediated transport of dietary lipids
categories.detailed_mechanism: Bile salts (from liver/gallbladder) emulsify lipids increasing surface area; pancreatic lipase + colipase hydrolyze triglycerides at sn-1 and sn-3 positions; mixed micelles (bile salts + fatty acids + monoglycerides + cholesterol) diffuse to enterocyte surface; lipids diffuse passively into cells; re-esterification and chylomicron assembly in ER/Golgi; exocytosis into lacteals for lymphatic transport

examples.explanation: 95% of bile salts reabsorbed in ileum via ASBT transporter; returned to liver via portal vein; resecreted into bile; pool recirculates 4-12 times per meal, minimizing need for de novo synthesis

image.caption: Enterohepatic circulation conserving bile salts for efficient lipid digestion

worked_examples.title: Calculating Glucose Absorption Rate via SGLT1
worked_examples.problem: If the luminal glucose concentration is 10 mM, intracellular glucose is 0.1 mM, the Na⁺ gradient provides 12 kJ/mol driving force, and SGLT1 transporter density is 10⁴ transporters/μm² with turnover rate 100 s⁻¹, estimate the maximum glucose absorption rate per cm² of intestinal surface.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate transporters per cm²: convert μm² to cm²
Calculate maximum transport rate per transporter
Convert to molar flux: Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
Account for concentration gradient and driving force: actual rate ~50% of V_max under physiological conditions

worked_examples.final_answer: Maximum theoretical glucose absorption via SGLT1 is ~0.166 μmol/cm²/s; physiological rate ~0.083 μmol/cm²/s (5 μmol/cm²/min), consistent with measured intestinal glucose uptake rates of 100-200 g/day in humans.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Transporter kinetics and surface area amplification in nutrient absorption

image.caption: Mathematical modeling of SGLT1-mediated glucose transport kinetics

worked_examples.title: Lipid Micelle Formation and Critical Micelle Concentration
worked_examples.problem: Given bile salt critical micelle concentration (CMC) = 3 mM, total bile salt concentration in duodenum = 15 mM, and lipid hydrolysis products = 10 mM, calculate the fraction of bile salts incorporated into micelles and estimate micelle number concentration assuming aggregation number N = 50.

step_by_step.explanation: Determine monomeric vs micellar bile salt concentrations
Calculate fraction of bile salts in micelles
Calculate micelle concentration using aggregation number N = 50
Estimate lipid solubilization capacity: each micelle can incorporate ~10 lipid molecules
Compare to available lipid hydrolysis products (10 mM): micelles can solubilize 24% of products at any moment; dynamic exchange allows complete absorption over time
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{Fraction solubilized} = \dfrac{2.4}{10} = 0.24$; continuous micelle recycling enables complete absorption

worked_examples.final_answer: 80% of bile salts form micelles at 240 μM concentration; micelles can solubilize ~2.4 mM lipids at equilibrium, with dynamic exchange enabling complete absorption of 10 mM lipid products during intestinal transit.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Critical micelle concentration and solubilization capacity in lipid absorption

image.caption: Bile salt aggregation and lipid solubilization in intestinal lumen

components.composition: Simple columnar epithelium with enterocytes, goblet cells, enteroendocrine cells; lamina propria with capillaries and lacteals; muscularis mucosae
components.function: Site of final digestion (brush-border enzymes) and nutrient absorption; barrier function via tight junctions; immune surveillance via Peyer's patches
components.key_feature: Villi increase surface area 10-fold; microvilli add another 20-fold; brush-border enzymes (lactase, peptidases) complete digestion at absorption site

image.caption: Cross-section showing villi, crypts, and cellular composition of absorptive mucosa

components.composition: Polarized exocrine cells with extensive rough ER and zymogen granules containing inactive proenzymes
components.function: Synthesize, store, and secrete digestive proenzymes (trypsinogen, chymotrypsinogen, procarboxypeptidase) and active enzymes (amylase, lipase, nucleases)
components.key_feature: Secretion regulated by CCK (enzyme-rich) and secretin (bicarbonate-rich); proenzymes prevent autodigestion; activated in duodenum by enterokinase

image.caption: Electron micrograph showing zymogen granules and secretory apparatus

components.composition: Polarized liver cells with sinusoidal (blood-facing) and canalicular (bile-facing) membrane domains
components.function: Synthesize bile acids from cholesterol; secrete bile containing bile salts, phospholipids, cholesterol, bilirubin into canaliculi for storage/concentration in gallbladder
components.key_feature: Enterohepatic circulation recycles 95% of bile salts; bile flow regulated by sphincter of Oddi and CCK-induced gallbladder contraction

image.caption: Polarized hepatocyte secreting bile components into canaliculi

components.composition: Myenteric (Auerbach's) plexus between muscle layers; submucosal (Meissner's) plexus in submucosa; >100 million neurons
components.function: Coordinate motility (peristalsis, segmentation), secretion, and blood flow via local reflexes; modulated by parasympathetic (stimulatory) and sympathetic (inhibitory) input
components.key_feature: Can function independently of CNS ('second brain'); integrates mechanical, chemical, and hormonal signals to optimize digestive function

image.caption: Myenteric and submucosal plexuses coordinating intestinal function

function.operation: The digestive system converts ingested food into absorbable nutrients through sequential mechanical processing, enzymatic hydrolysis, and transporter-mediated absorption, while eliminating indigestible residues
function.energy: Digestive processes consume ~10% of basal metabolic rate (thermic effect of food); active transport (SGLT1, PEPT1) requires ATP for Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase maintenance of ion gradients
function.mechanism: Luminal chemoreceptors and mechanoreceptors trigger enteric reflexes and hormone release (gastrin, CCK, secretin); hormones act on target organs to coordinate secretion and motility; brush-border enzymes and transporters complete digestion/absorption at epithelial surface

image.caption: Neural, hormonal, and local control mechanisms coordinating digestive function

cycle.operation: Sight, smell, taste, or thought of food activates vagal nuclei
cycle.purpose: Prepare digestive organs for incoming food via anticipatory secretion

image.caption: Vagal stimulation of gastric acid and pancreatic enzyme secretion before food arrival

cycle.operation: Food distension and peptides in stomach stimulate gastrin release and vagovagal reflexes
cycle.purpose: Maximize gastric digestion via acid secretion, pepsin activation, and mixing motility

image.caption: Local and hormonal control of gastric secretion and motility

cycle.operation: Chyme entering duodenum triggers CCK, secretin, GIP release and enterogastric reflexes
cycle.purpose: Coordinate pancreatic secretion, bile release, and gastric emptying to match intestinal processing capacity

image.caption: Hormonal and neural feedback regulating gastric emptying and pancreaticobiliary secretion

cycle.operation: Brush-border enzymes complete digestion; transporters move nutrients into enterocytes; chylomicrons package lipids for lymphatic transport
cycle.purpose: Efficiently transfer digestion products to blood/lymph for systemic distribution

image.caption: Transport pathways for carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids across intestinal epithelium

cycle.operation: Water and electrolyte reabsorption; bacterial fermentation of undigested carbohydrates; feces formation and storage
cycle.purpose: Conserve water, recover electrolytes, and prepare waste for elimination

image.caption: Water absorption, bacterial fermentation, and fecal compaction in colon

process_flow.description: Food is masticated, mixed with saliva containing amylase and lingual lipase, and formed into bolus for swallowing
process_flow.mechanism: Mastication reduces particle size; salivary amylase initiates starch hydrolysis ($\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\alpha\text{-amylase}} \text{Maltose} + \text{Dextrins}$); lingual lipase begins lipid digestion; swallowing reflex propels bolus to esophagus
process_flow.result: Bolus with increased surface area and initiated carbohydrate digestion enters stomach

image.caption: Mastication, salivary secretion, and bolus formation

process_flow.process: Gastric Storage, Mixing, and Protein Digestion
process_flow.description: Stomach stores food, mixes with gastric juice, and initiates protein hydrolysis while regulating emptying to duodenum
process_flow.mechanism: Parietal cells secrete HCl (pH 1.5-2.5) activating pepsinogen $\rightarrow$ pepsin; chief cells secrete pepsinogen; G-cells release gastrin; mixing waves churn chyme; pylorus meters particles <2 mm to duodenum
process_flow.result: Acidified chyme with partially digested proteins enters duodenum at rate matching intestinal processing capacity

image.caption: Gastric secretion, mixing, and controlled emptying

process_flow.description: Acidic chyme triggers secretin release; pancreatic bicarbonate neutralizes pH; CCK stimulates enzyme secretion; enterokinase activates trypsinogen
process_flow.mechanism: Secretin $\rightarrow$ pancreatic duct cells secrete $\text{HCO}_3^-$ raising pH to 7.5-8.5; CCK $\rightarrow$ acinar cells secrete proteases, amylase, lipase; enterokinase on brush border converts trypsinogen $\rightarrow$ trypsin, which activates other proenzymes
process_flow.result: Optimal pH and active enzyme cocktail for complete macromolecule hydrolysis

image.caption: Hormonal regulation of pancreatic secretion and proenzyme activation cascade

process_flow.description: Brush-border enzymes complete hydrolysis; transporters move nutrients into enterocytes; lipids packaged into chylomicrons
process_flow.mechanism: Disaccharidases, peptidases finalize digestion; SGLT1/PEPT1/amino acid transporters absorb monomers; fatty acids/monoglycerides diffuse into cells, re-esterified, packaged into chylomicrons for lymphatic transport
process_flow.result: Nutrients enter portal blood (carbohydrates, proteins) or lymph (lipids) for systemic distribution

image.caption: Cellular pathways for carbohydrate, protein, and lipid absorption

process_flow.description: Terminal ileum absorbs remaining nutrients, bile salts, and vitamin B₁₂; lipids/carbohydrates here trigger feedback inhibiting gastric emptying
process_flow.mechanism: ASBT transporter reabsorbs bile salts; cubilin receptor absorbs B₁₂-intrinsic factor complex; nutrient presence stimulates PYY/GLP-1 release slowing proximal motility
process_flow.result: Conservation of bile salts and B₁₂; regulation of transit time to maximize absorption efficiency

image.caption: Specialized ileal functions and hormonal feedback regulation

process_flow.process: Colonic Water Recovery and Feces Formation
process_flow.description: Large intestine absorbs water/electrolytes; bacteria ferment undigested carbohydrates; feces stored in rectum until defecation
process_flow.mechanism: Na⁺/water absorption via ENaC channels; bacterial fermentation produces SCFAs (absorbed for energy) and gases; mass movements propel feces to rectum; defecation reflex coordinates sphincter relaxation
process_flow.result: Water conservation, energy recovery from fiber, and elimination of indigestible residues

image.caption: Water absorption, bacterial fermentation, and defecation reflex

process_types.energy_profile: Low energy demand; migrating motor complex (MMC) clears residual contents
process_types.direction: Cyclic motility pattern every 90-120 min: quiescence $\rightarrow$ irregular activity $\rightarrow$ propagated peristalsis
process_types.function: Sweep undigested material and bacteria toward colon to prevent overgrowth; prepare tract for next meal
goals: Prevent bacterial overgrowth in small intestine
process_types.energy_source: Basal metabolic rate; MMC regulated by motilin hormone

primary_example.explanation: High-amplitude peristaltic wave originating in stomach propagates to ileum at 5 cm/min, clearing lumen; mediated by motilin and enteric cholinergic neurons

image.caption: MMC propagation pattern and hormonal regulation during fasting

examples.explanation: Gallbladder fills with concentrated bile; minimal secretion; enterohepatic circulation maintains bile salt pool without meal stimulation

image.caption: Gallbladder storage and minimal bile flow in interdigestive state

process_types.energy_profile: Increased energy demand: splanchnic blood flow rises 30-50%; thermic effect of food ~10% of meal calories
process_types.direction: Segmentation dominates in small intestine for mixing; regulated gastric emptying matches duodenal processing capacity
process_types.function: Maximize nutrient digestion and absorption while preventing osmotic diarrhea or mucosal damage from unprocessed chyme
process_types.energy_source: Increased splanchnic metabolism; postprandial insulin promotes nutrient uptake

primary_example.explanation: Carbohydrates, proteins, lipids each trigger specific hormonal responses: glucose $\rightarrow$ GIP/insulin; amino acids $\rightarrow$ gastrin; fats $\rightarrow$ CCK; integrated response optimizes enzyme secretion and motility

image.caption: Hormonal and neural coordination of digestive secretions for complex meals

examples.explanation: Fat in duodenum strongly stimulates CCK, slowing gastric emptying (ileal brake) to allow time for emulsification and lipase action; gallbladder contracts to deliver bile

image.caption: CCK-mediated coordination of gastric emptying, pancreatic secretion, and gallbladder contraction

process_types.energy_profile: Variable; often increased energy demand due to compensatory mechanisms or underlying disease
process_types.direction: Impaired digestion or absorption leading to nutrient loss in feces; osmotic or secretory diarrhea
process_types.function: Illustrate consequences of specific digestive component failure
process_types.energy_source: Often inefficient due to nutrient loss and compensatory hyperphagia

primary_example.explanation: Gluten-triggered immune response damages enterocytes; villous blunting reduces surface area; brush-border enzyme loss impairs final digestion; malabsorption of multiple nutrients causes diarrhea, weight loss, deficiencies

image.caption: Histological changes and functional consequences of gluten-induced enteropathy

examples.explanation: Reduced enzyme secretion (chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis) impairs macromolecule hydrolysis; undigested nutrients cause osmotic diarrhea and steatorrhea; treated with pancreatic enzyme replacement

image.caption: Consequences of enzyme deficiency on nutrient digestion and stool composition

process_types.energy_profile: Transient increase during adaptation; returns to baseline once enzymes/transporters upregulated
process_types.direction: Upregulation of specific digestive components in response to chronic nutrient exposure
process_types.function: Optimize digestive efficiency for prevailing diet composition
goals: Match enzyme/transporter expression to nutrient load
Maintain absorption efficiency across dietary variations
Prevent osmotic stress from unabsorbed nutrients
process_types.energy_source: Protein synthesis for enzyme/transporter upregulation; regulated at transcriptional and post-translational levels

primary_example.explanation: Most mammals downregulate lactase after weaning; human populations with dairy domestication history maintain lactase expression into adulthood via genetic regulatory variants; enables continued lactose digestion

image.caption: Genetic and physiological basis of lactase persistence in human populations

examples.explanation: Chronic high protein intake upregulates intestinal peptide transporters (PEPT1) and amino acid transporters; enhances absorption capacity and reduces nitrogen loss

image.caption: Dietary regulation of intestinal nutrient transporter expression

sections.relationship: The digestive system interfaces with the circulatory system for nutrient distribution, with the endocrine system via GI hormones and incretins regulating metabolism, with the nervous system via enteric and autonomic control, and with the immune system via gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) maintaining barrier integrity
sections.purpose: To convert ingested food into absorbable nutrients through coordinated mechanical and chemical processes, absorb these nutrients into blood or lymph for systemic distribution, and eliminate indigestible residues while maintaining mucosal barrier function and immune surveillance

activation.definition: Digestive processes are activated by cephalic stimuli (sight/smell of food), gastric distension and chemoreception, and intestinal nutrient detection, triggering neural reflexes and hormone release (gastrin, CCK, secretin, GIP)
activation.effect: Coordinates secretion of digestive enzymes, bicarbonate, and bile; regulates motility patterns and gastric emptying rate; upregulates absorptive transporters to match nutrient load

image.caption: Integration of neural, hormonal, and luminal signals initiating and regulating digestive function

key_properties.explanation: Hierarchical folding (plicae $\times 3$, villi $\times 10$, microvilli $\times 20$) increases absorptive area ~600-fold to ~200-300 m²; critical for efficient nutrient uptake despite short transit time

image.caption: Quantitative contribution of each structural level to total absorptive surface

key_properties.explanation: Enzymes function at pH matching anatomical location: salivary amylase (6.7-7.0), pepsin (1.5-2.5), pancreatic enzymes (7.5-8.5); maintained by regulated acid/bicarbonate secretion

image.caption: Luminal pH profile and corresponding enzyme activity optima

key_properties.explanation: Nutrient absorption uses specific transporters (SGLT1, PEPT1, amino acid systems) with finite capacity; high luminal concentrations can saturate transporters, limiting absorption rate

image.caption: Michaelis-Menten kinetics of nutrient transporters and physiological implications

key_properties.explanation: Bile salts and vitamin B₁₂ undergo enterohepatic circulation: absorbed in ileum, returned to liver via portal vein, resecreted; conserves limited pools and minimizes synthetic demand

image.caption: Recycling of bile salts and B₁₂ between intestine and liver

summary: The digestive system transforms food into absorbable nutrients through sequential mechanical and chemical processes along a specialized tubular tract. Mechanical digestion (mastication, gastric mixing, intestinal segmentation) increases surface area for enzymatic action. Chemical digestion employs compartment-specific enzymes: salivary/pancreatic amylase hydrolyzes carbohydrates; pepsin and pancreatic proteases cleave proteins; pancreatic lipase with bile salt emulsification digests lipids. Final hydrolysis occurs at the brush border via disaccharidases and peptidases. Absorption leverages hierarchical surface area amplification (~600-fold via plicae, villi, microvilli) and specialized transporters: SGLT1 cotransports glucose/galactose with Na⁺; PEPT1 absorbs di/tripeptides with H⁺; lipids diffuse after micellar solubilization and are packaged into chylomicrons for lymphatic transport. Regulation integrates cephalic (anticipatory), gastric (distension/peptide-triggered), and intestinal (nutrient-sensing) phases via enteric reflexes and hormones (gastrin, CCK, secretin, GIP) that coordinate secretion, motility, and gastric emptying. The small intestine absorbs ~90% of nutrients; the ileum recovers bile salts and B₁₂ via enterohepatic circulation; the colon reclaims water and electrolytes while bacterial fermentation extracts energy from fiber. Pathologies like celiac disease (villous atrophy), pancreatic insufficiency (enzyme deficiency), or lactase non-persistence illustrate consequences of specific component failure. Understanding digestive physiology is essential for managing malabsorption, optimizing nutrition, developing enzyme replacement therapies, and appreciating gut-brain-endocrine integration in metabolic health.
